items.title: Esophageal biopsy: diagnostic standard
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis by esophageal biopsy

  • ≥6 biopsies of the proximal and distal esophagus are required to increase sensitivity.
  • The sample should be taken from areas with endoscopic changes (rings, grooves, exudates) but also from normal-appearing mucosa.
  • Tissue eosinophilia is patchy; a single biopsy can give false negatives.
  • Avoid biopsies of the lower third exclusively, as it can be confused with gastroesophageal reflux disease.
  • The pathology report should include the maximum eosinophil count per high-power field (HPF).

items.title: Key histological threshold
items.contentHtml:
≥15 eosinophils per high power field

  • The diagnostic threshold is ≥15 eosinophils/HPF in at least one esophageal biopsy.
  • This cutoff differentiates EoE from other causes of esophageal eosinophilia (gastroesophageal reflux disease, infections, Crohn's disease, eosinophilic hyperplasia).
  • The same threshold is used in pediatrics, although some authors suggest >20/HPF for greater specificity.
  • Peripheral blood eosinophilia is not required; the absence does not rule out EoE.
  • The presence of eosinophilic microabscesses and degranulation supports the diagnosis.

items.foco: Symptoms according to the patient's age
items.title: Variable symptoms by age
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms according to patient's age

  • In young children: food refusal, vomiting, abdominal pain, failure to thrive.
  • In adolescents: intermittent dysphagia, food impaction, atypical heartburn.
  • In adults: progressive solid dysphagia (90%), food impaction (30-50%), pain retrosternal.
  • Reflux symptoms may overlap with gastroesophageal reflux disease, but the response to PPI is partial or absent.
  • Alimentary impaction is a high-risk marker and requires urgent endoscopy.

items.title: Dysphagia and impaction: typical presentation
items.contentHtml:
Dysphagia and food impaction

  • Dysphagia is the most common symptom in adults (>80%), predominantly for solids.
  • Food impaction occurs due to stenosis or decreased esophageal distensibility.
  • In endoscopy, impaction is usually located in the proximal or middle third.
  • History of intermittent dysphagia with episodes Impaction should raise suspicion of EoE.
  • The differential diagnosis includes Schatzki ring, achalasia and peptic stenosis.

items.title: Response to PPI: diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
Failure to respond to PPI

  • Before confirming EoE, a therapeutic trial with PPI (double dose for 8 weeks) should be performed.
  • The failure to respond to PPI (persistence of symptoms and eosinophilia ≥15/HPF) supports EoE.
  • Up to 30% of patients with eosinophilia esophageal tissue responds to PPI (overlapping gastroesophageal reflux disease-EoE phenotype).
  • It is not recommended to suspend PPI before biopsy if the patient is receiving treatment.
  • Lack of response to PPI is a diagnostic criterion in current guidelines (AGREE, ACG).

items.title: Swallowed topical corticosteroids: first line
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with swallowed topical corticosteroids

  • First line pharmacology: swallowed fluticasone (440-880 mcg/day in adults, adjusted in children) or viscous budesonide (1-2 mg/day).
  • They are administered by swallowing the contents without rinsing or eating afterwards for 30 minutes.
  • The histological response is evaluated after 8-12 weeks with new endoscopy and biopsies.
  • Do not use systemic corticosteroids except in severe cases or non-dilatable strictures.
  • Local adverse effects: esophageal candidiasis (5-10%), rarely adrenal suppression.

items.title: Elimination diet: non-pharmacological strategy
items.contentHtml:
Empirical elimination diet

  • The most commonly used empirical elimination diet is that of 6 foods: milk, wheat, egg, soy, nuts, fish/shellfish.
  • It is maintained for 6-8 weeks, followed by endoscopy with biopsies to assess response.
  • If there is histological remission (≤15 eos/HPF), foods are reintroduced one by one with surveillance.
  • The allergy-directed elimination diet (patch or prick test) is less effective than the empirical one.
  • It is a first-line alternative in children; In adults it is preferred as a second option after topical corticosteroids.

items.foco: Endoscopy with rings and linear grooves
items.title: Characteristic endoscopic findings
items.contentHtml:
Endoscopy with rings and linear grooves

  • Typical endoscopic findings include concentric rings (esophagus in trachea), linear grooves and whitish exudates (microabscesses).
  • Others: mucosal edema, loss of vascular pattern, stenosis and fragility (paper sign). crepe).
  • The endoscopic classification EREFS (Edema, Rings, Exudates, Furrows, Strictures) scores each finding from 0 to 2.
  • The presence of rings and grooves has high specificity (>90%) for active EoE.
  • The mucosa can be normal in up to 10-15% of cases, it does not exclude disease.

items.foco: Exclusion of other causes of eosinophilia
items.title: Differential diagnosis of esophageal eosinophilia
items.contentHtml:
Exclusion of other causes of eosinophilia

  • Should be excluded gastroesophageal reflux disease (response to PPI, mild distal eosinophilia), Crohn's disease with esophageal involvement, infections (parasites, fungi).
  • Others: hypereosinophilic syndrome (systemic eosinophilia >1500 eos/μL), vasculitis (Churg-Strauss), achalasia and scleroderma.
  • Eosinophilic gastroenteritis affects the stomach and small intestine, not just the esophagus.
  • History of atopy, food allergies and response to elimination diet supports EoE.
  • Allergy panel recommended (specific IgE, prick test) to identify triggers.

items.title: Atopy and allergies: common comorbidity
items.contentHtml:
Association with atopy and allergies

  • Up to 70-80% of patients with EoE have a history of atopy (asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema).
  • Food sensitization (milk, wheat, egg) is common, mediated by IgE or delayed cellular response.
  • The allergy to cow's milk is the most common trigger in children.
  • EoE is not a pure IgE-dependent disease; Th2 inflammation with eosinophils predominates.
  • Allergological evaluation helps guide the targeted elimination diet, although the empirical diet is more effective.

items.title: Endoscopic dilation: management of stenosis
items.contentHtml:
Endoscopic dilation in stenosis

  • Indicated in symptomatic esophageal stenosis that does not respond to medical treatment (corticosteroids + diet).
  • It is performed with a balloon or Savary-Gilliard dilators; the risk of perforation is greater than in peptic stenosis (5-10%).
  • It should not be performed on highly inflamed mucosa; It is preferred to treat first with topical corticosteroids.
  • Recurrence of stenosis is common if the underlying inflammation is not controlled.
  • Dilation can improve dysphagia but does not modify the histology.

items.title: Recurrence after stopping treatment
items.contentHtml:
Frequent recurrence when treatment is suspended

  • EoE is a chronic disease with a high rate of recurrence when topical corticosteroids or elimination diet are suspended.
  • Up to 90% of patients present histological and symptomatic relapse in 6-12 months after discontinuing treatment.
  • Required long-term maintenance treatment: low doses of topical corticosteroids (budesonide 0.5 mg/day) or partial elimination diet.
  • Periodic monitoring with endoscopy and biopsies is indicated to evaluate activity.
  • Sustained remission without treatment is exceptional; most need ongoing therapy.
